Chapter 15  
Dynamic Programming  
Masroor Hussain  
Professor  
Faculty of Computer Science and Engineering  
1
Dynamic programming  
• Like the divide-and-conquer method  
– Divide-and-conquer algorithms partition the problem into disjoint  
subproblems, solve the subproblems recursively, and then combine their  
solutions to solve the original problem.  
• Dynamic programming  
– It applies when the subproblems are overlapped, means when  
subproblems share subsubproblems.  
• Method includes four steps:  
1. Characterize the structure of an optimal solution.  
2. Recursively define the value of an optimal solution.  
3. Compute the value of an optimal solution, typically in a bottom-up  
fashion.  
4. Construct an optimal solution from computed information.  
3
Rod Cutting Example  
The price pi in dollars that Serling Enterprises charges for a  
rod of length i inches.  
•
• The rod-cutting problem is the following. Given a rod of  
length n inches and a table of prices pi for i = 1, 2, …, n,  
determine the maximum revenue rn obtainable by cutting up  
the rod and selling the pieces.  
• We can cut up a rod of length n in 2n - 1 different ways  
4
Rod cutting example ...  
• We see that cutting a 4-inch rod into two 2-inch pieces  
produces revenue p2 + p2 = 5 + 5 = 10, which is optimal.  
5
Rod Cutting Example …

Matrix Multiplication  
13  
Matrix Chain Multiplication  
Given a a sequence (chain) <A1, A2, ..., An> of n matrices to  
be multiplied  
•
•
E.g. Multiplying A1 A2 A3 A4 have five ways:  
(A1(A2(A3 A4)))  
(A1 ((A2A3)A4))  
((A1 A2)(A3A4))  
(((A1 (A2A3))A4)  
(((A1 A2)A3)A4)  
–
–
–
–
–
• Total number of parentheses (Catalan number)  
–
14

1. Characterize the structure of an optimal solution.  
2. Recursively define the value of an optimal solution.  
3. Compute the value of an optimal solution.  
4. Construct an optimal solution from computed information.

18  
Constructing an optimal solution  
19  
Subtleties  
• Unweighted shortest path:  
– Find a path from u to consisting of the fewest edges. Such a path must be  
simple, since removing a cycle from a path produces a path with fewer  
edges.  
• Unweighted longest simple path:  
– Find a simple path from u to consisting of the most edges. We need to  
include the requirement of simplicity because otherwise we can traverse a  
cycle as many times as we like to create paths with an arbitrarily large  
number of edges.  
20
